narrative: Open mic night at The Rail. Cassidy’s idea, six weeks in the making, and the turnout is better than she hoped — twenty people plus the regulars, a guitarist from Laurel, two poets, and then Eli, who put his name on the list as a joke and is now standing at the microphone with a single page of something he wrote this morning. The room is doing that thing where everyone pretends not to pay attention while absolutely paying attention. He reads. It’s about standing in a parking lot at dawn and watching a woman load groceries into her car and the way the light caught the fog of her breath. It’s two minutes long. When he finishes there’s a beat of silence and then real applause, not polite applause, and Eli sits down with his hands shaking and his heart going and thinks: oh. That’s what it feels like. Out on the Yellowstone, Travis landed a twenty-two-inch brown trout on a size-sixteen pheasant tail, held it in the current until it kicked free, and is now sitting on the bank in the last light with no desire to cast again. Sometimes the river gives you exactly one thing and you know to stop.
events: The Rail Bar hosts an open mic night — Eli reads something he wrote and it’s good, and the room goes quiet in the right way
Travis catches a massive brown trout on the Yellowstone and releases it, then sits on the bank for an hour watching the water
